mainTopic: (continues) הפקדה אצל האם · אפוטרופוס ובקרא · הפקדת כשותא
overview: (1) A case where a shomer deposited money with his mother who lost it, leading to a ruling that the shomer is liable for the deposit but the mother is exempt. (2) A case of an orphan's ox dying while with a herdsman, discussing liability and the status of the herdsman as a shomer sachar. (3) A case of deposited cheese where the shomer's servant took the wrong batch, discussing liability and loss. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: A case where a shomer deposited money with his mother who lost it, leading to a ruling that the shomer is liable for the deposit but the mother is exempt.
A case of an orphan's ox dying while with a herdsman, discussing liability and the status of the herdsman as a shomer sachar.
A case of deposited cheese where the shomer's servant took the wrong batch, discussing liability and loss. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: Challenge: The mother should pay
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara suggests ruling that the mother should pay, but she defends herself by saying she didn't know the money wasn't hers, so she didn't bury it.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara proposes that the mother should pay, but she raises a defense based on her lack of knowledge about the money's ownership. This is a challenge to the suggestion that the mother should pay.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara shifts focus to the mother, suggesting she should pay. However, she argues that she didn't know the money wasn't hers, so she didn't take the optimal safeguarding measure of burying it. This defense relies on the assumption that she would have acted differently if she knew the money wasn't hers.
steps.whatToRemember: The mother's defense relies on her lack of knowledge about the money's ownership.
steps.confusionAlert: The mother is not the original bailee; she is a secondary custodian.
steps.whyThisMatters: This defense highlights the importance of knowledge in determining liability for safeguarding.

phrases.english: Let us say to his mother:
Go and pay, she said:

phrases.aramaic: לָא אֲמַר לִי דְּלָאו דִּידֵיהּ נִינְהוּ דְּאֶקְבְּרִינְהוּ.
phrases.english: He did not tell me that they are not his, that I should bury them.
phrases.notes: Burying money was a common method of safeguarding funds.

steps.triggerLanguage: נֵימָא לַהּ לְאִמֵּיהּ: זִילִי שַׁלִּימִי, אָמְרָה: לָא אֲמַר לִי דְּלָא דִידֵיהּ נִינְהוּ דְּאֶקְבְּרִינְהוּ.

rashi.hebrew: נימא לה לאימיה - שתשלם בשביל שלא קברתו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the challenge to the mother is that she should pay because she failed to bury the money.

steps.title: Challenge: Why didn't the bailee tell her?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks why the bailee didn't tell his mother the money wasn't his, but he argues that telling her would have made her even more careful.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara presses the bailee on why he didn't inform his mother about the money's ownership, but he argues that doing so would have increased her caution. This is a challenge to the bailee's silence.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara challenges the bailee's silence, asking why he didn't tell his mother the money wasn't his. He responds that telling her would have made her even more careful, implying that his silence was actually beneficial for safeguarding the money.

keyTerms.meaning: why did you not say to her
she is even more careful with them

steps.whatToRemember: The bailee's silence was intended to increase his mother's caution.
steps.confusionAlert: The bailee's argument is counterintuitive; silence usually reduces caution.
steps.whyThisMatters: This argument highlights the complexity of determining liability when information is withheld.

phrases.aramaic: נֵימָא לֵיהּ: אַמַּאי לָא אֲמַרְתְּ לַהּ?
phrases.english: Let us say to him: Why did you not say to her?
He said: All the more so,
for if I had told her they are mine —
she would be more careful with them.

steps.triggerLanguage: נֵימָא לֵיהּ: אַמַּאי לָא אֲמַרְתְּ לַהּ? אָמַר: כָּל שֶׁכֵּן, דְּכִי אָמֵינָא לַהּ דְּדִידִי נִינְהוּ – טְפֵי מִזְדַּהֲרָא בְּהוּ.

tosafot.hebrew: נימא ליה לאפוטרופוס זיל שלים אמר [אנא] לבקרא מסרתיה - משמע דאם לא היתה זאת הטענה הוי פושע וחייב וקשה דבפ' ד' וה' (ב"ק דף לט. ושם ד"ה דאי) אמרינן מעליית מאן מעליית יתומים דאי אמרת מעליית אפוטרופוס ממנעי ולא עבדי אלמא פטורים מן הפשיעה וממתני' דהנזקין (גיטין דף נב.) דקתני מינהו ב"ד לא ישבע מינהו אבי יתומים ישבע אבא שאול אומר חילוף הדברים אין להוכיח דחייב בפשיעה די"ל דהשבועה לא הויא שלא פשע אלא שלא עכב כלום משלהם משום דמורי היתירא דטרח קמייהו וי"ל דודאי אפוטרופוס חייב בפשיעה כדמשמע הכא ולא ממנע כיון שהחזיקוהו בנאמן אבל התם שלא מנוהו לצורך היתומים אלא לתקנת העולם שלא יזוקו משורם התם דוקא חיישינן דלמא ממנעי והכי איתא בירושלמי פרק הנזקין דקאמר אתיא דר' יוחנן כרבנן בתמיה פירוש כרבנן דאמרי מנוהו ב"ד לא ישבע אפילו סבר כאבא שאול בעי ההוא בר נש מיתן בגין דמתקרי מהימן פי' רוצה ליתן אם יפשע ולא ימנע כיון שנקרא נאמן אבל הכא שלא הועמד אלא לתקנת העולם חיישינן דלמא ממנע אתיא דרבי יוסי בר חנינא כאבא שאול בתמיה אפילו סבר כרבנן אדם מבריח עצמו מן השבועה ואין מבריח עצמו מן התשלומין פירוש כשיודע שפשע רוצה לשלם אבל אם נאבד אינו חפץ לשבע שלא פשע דא"כ חושדין אותו:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the bailee's argument implies he would be considered פושע if he didn't use this reasoning, and reconciles this with the Yerushalmi by distinguishing between a court-appointed guardian for the orphans' benefit (who is liable for פשיעה) and one appointed for public welfare (who is exempt from פשיעה to prevent people from refusing the role).

steps.title: Rava's resolution: Oaths for both parties
steps.stepSummary: Rava resolves the case by requiring both the bailee and his mother to take oaths, after which the bailee is exempt from payment.
steps.whatsHappening: Rava provides a resolution to the case by requiring both parties to take oaths, which exempts the bailee from payment. This is a resolution to the dialectic.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rava's resolution involves both the bailee and his mother taking oaths. The bailee swears that he gave the money to his mother, and the mother swears that she placed it in a chest and it was stolen. After these oaths, the bailee is exempt from payment.
steps.whatToRemember: Rava's resolution involves oaths for both the bailee and his mother.
steps.confusionAlert: The oaths are not about liability but about the facts of the case.
steps.whyThisMatters: This resolution highlights the role of oaths in resolving complex cases where liability is unclear.

phrases.english: Rather, Rava said:

phrases.aramaic: מִשְׁתְּבַע אִיהוּ דְּהָנְהוּ זוּזֵי אַשְׁלְמִינְהוּ לְאִימֵּיהּ,
phrases.english: He takes an oath that he gave those coins to his mother,

phrases.aramaic: וּמִשְׁתַּבְעָא אִימֵּיהּ דְּהָנְהוּ זוּזֵי אוֹתְבִינְהוּ בְּקַרְטְלִיתָא וְאִיגְּנוּב –
phrases.english: and his mother takes an oath that she placed those coins in the chest and they were stolen –
and he is exempt.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא אָמַר רָבָא: מִשְׁתַּבַּע אִיהוּ דְּהָנֵי זוזֵי אַשְׁלְמִינְהוּ לְאִמֵּיהּ, וּמִשְׁתַּבְעָא אִמֵּיהּ דְּהָנֵי זוזֵי אוֹתְבִינְהוּ בְּקַרְטְלִיתָא וְאִיגְנֵב – וּפָטוּר.

steps.title: The case of the steward and the toothless ox
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara presents a case where a steward buys an ox for orphans, gives it to a herdsman, and the ox dies because it has no teeth to eat.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a case involving an אַפּוֹטְרוֹפָא, יַתְמֵי, a בַּקָּרָא, and a defective ox. This is a setup for a liability puzzle.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ox died because it lacked teeth to eat. The steward bought it for the orphans and handed it to the herdsman. The question is who bears the loss.
steps.whatToRemember: The ox died because it lacked teeth to eat, not from negligence in guarding.
steps.confusionAlert: The defect was hidden; no one knew the ox couldn't eat.
steps.whyThisMatters: This case tests the limits of a שומר שכר's liability when the loss stems from a hidden defect rather than negligence.

phrases.aramaic: הָהוּא אַפּוֹטְרוֹפָּא דְיַתְמֵי דִּזְבַן לְהוּ תּוֹרָא
phrases.english: That steward of orphans who bought for them an ox
for the orphans and gave it to the cowherd.

phrases.aramaic: לָא הֲווֹ לֵיהּ כַּכֵּי וְשִׁינֵּי לְמֵיכַל, וּמִית.
phrases.english: It did not have molars and teeth to eat, and it died.
Rami bar Chama said:
How should judges judge this judgment?

steps.triggerLanguage: הָהוּא אַפּוֹטְרוֹפָא דְיַתְמֵי דִזְבַן לְהוּ תוֹרָא לְיַתְמֵי וּמַסְרֵיהּ לְבַקָּרָא. לָא הֲווֹ לֵיהּ כַּכֵּי וְשִׁנֵּי לְמֵיכַל, וּמִית. אָמַר רָמֵי בַר חָמָא: הֵיכִי נְדַיְינוּ דַּיָּינֵי לְהַאי דִּינָא?

steps.title: Pressing the steward to pay
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explores the initial line of questioning: telling the steward to pay, and his defense that he handed it to the herdsman.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara simulates the court's first attempt to assign liability to the steward. This is a setup for the chain of custody.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The court asks the steward to pay. He deflects, saying he gave it to the herdsman. This sets up the chain of custody and potential liability.
steps.whatToRemember: The steward's defense is that he transferred custody to the herdsman.
steps.confusionAlert: The steward is not claiming the ox was fine; he's claiming he passed responsibility.
steps.whyThisMatters: Establishes the steward's role as a middleman, not the primary caretaker.

phrases.english: Let them say to the steward:
Go pay, he said:
I gave it to the cowherd.

steps.triggerLanguage: נֵימָא לֵיהּ לְאַפּוֹטְרוֹפָא: זִיל שַׁלֵּים, אָמַר: אֲנָא לְבַקָּרָא מְסַרְתֵּיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: לבקרא מסרתיה - היה לו להודיעני שאינו אוכל:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the steward's claim of transferring the item to the herdsman implies he should have informed the owner that the herdsman is not liable for consumption, thereby shifting the burden of proof.

steps.title: Pressing the herdsman to pay
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explores the next line of questioning: telling the herdsman to pay, and his defense that he fed it with other oxen and didn't know it wouldn't eat.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara simulates the court's attempt to assign liability to the herdsman. This is a setup for the herdsman's defense.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The court asks the herdsman to pay. He defends himself by saying he treated it like any other ox (placed it with others, threw food), and didn't know it wouldn't eat. This highlights the hidden nature of the defect.

keyTerms.meaning: I placed it with other oxen

steps.whatToRemember: The herdsman's defense is lack of knowledge of the defect.
steps.confusionAlert: The herdsman is claiming he fulfilled his duty of care; the defect was unknown.
steps.whyThisMatters: Sets up the core tension: is a שומר שכר liable for hidden defects he didn't know about?

phrases.english: Let us say to the cowherd:
Go pay, he said:
I placed it with the oxen,
I threw food to it,
We did not know that it did not eat.

steps.triggerLanguage: נֵימָא לֵיהּ לְבַקָּרָא: זִיל שַׁלֵּים, אָמַר: אֲנָא בַּהֲדֵי תוֹרֵי אוֹקֵימְתֵּיהּ, אוּכְלָא שְׁדַאי לֵיהּ, לָא הֲוָה יָדַעְינָא דְלָא אֲכַל.

steps.title: The herdsman is a שומר שכר of the orphans
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the herdsman's defense: as a paid bailee of the orphans, he should have examined the ox to ensure it wasn't suffering.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara presses the herdsman: his status as a שומר שכר imposes a duty of examination. This is a challenge to the herdsman's defense.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The herdsman is a שומר שכר for the orphans. A שומר שכר is liable for negligence. Not checking if the ox was eating could be seen as negligence. The Gemara asks: why isn't he liable for failing to examine?
steps.whatToRemember: A שומר שכר has a duty to examine for potential loss.
steps.confusionAlert: The duty to examine is specific to a שומר שכר, not a שומר חינם.
steps.whyThisMatters: This is the core legal pressure: does the duty to examine extend to hidden defects?

phrases.aramaic: מִכְּדֵי בַּקָּרָא שׁוֹמֵר שָׂכָר דְּיַתְמֵי הוּא!
phrases.english: After all, the cowherd is a paid bailee of the orphans!

phrases.aramaic: אִיבְּעִי לֵיהּ לְעַיּוֹנֵי אִי אִיכָּא פְּסֵידָא דְיַתְמֵי.
phrases.english: He was required to examine if there is loss of the orphans.
Here too, and here with what are we dealing?
That there is no loss of the orphans,

phrases.aramaic: דְּאַשְׁכְּחוּהּ לְמָרֵיהּ דְּתוֹרָא וּשְׁקוּל יַתְמֵי זוּזֵי מִינֵּיהּ.
phrases.english: That they found the owner of the ox and the orphans took coins from him.

steps.triggerLanguage: מִכְדֵּי בַּקָּרָא שׁוֹמֵר שָׂכָר דְיַתְמֵי הוּא! אִיבְּעִי לֵיהּ לְעַיּוֹנֵי אִי אִיכָּא פְּסֵידָא דְיַתְמֵי.

rashi.hebrew: איבעי ליה לעיוני - אם אוכל אם לאו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the duty to examine (לעיוני) refers specifically to checking whether the ox was eating the orphans' produce.

rashi.hebrew: אי איכא פסידא ליתמי כו' - תירוצא הוא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Gemara's statement about potential loss to the orphans (פסידא דיתמי) serves as the answer (תירוצא) to the previous difficulty.
רש״י identifies the owner of the ox (מריה דתורא) as the seller of the animal.

rashi.hebrew: ושקול דמייהו - דאמרי ליה מקח טעות הוא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the orphans can recover their money (שקול דמייהו) by claiming the purchase was a mistake (מקח טעות) due to the ox's hidden defect.

steps.title: Who is claiming compensation?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks: if the orphans got their money back, who is actually claiming compensation from the herdsman?
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara presses: if the orphans aren't at a loss, who is suing the herdsman? This is a challenge to the plaintiff's standing.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The previous resolution said the orphans got their money back. So why is the herdsman being sued? The Gemara asks who the plaintiff is.
steps.whatToRemember: The orphans aren't the ones suing.
steps.confusionAlert: The plaintiff is not the orphans.
steps.whyThisMatters: This shifts the focus to the seller's claim.

phrases.english: Rather, who is claiming?
The master of the ox is claiming.
He should have informed us!
What do we inform him?
From knowing, he knew that it was a mistaken purchase.
In a case of a trader who buys from here and sells to there,

phrases.aramaic: הִלְכָּךְ מִישְׁתְּבַע אִיהוּ דְּלָא הֲוָה יָדַע,
phrases.english: Therefore, he is made to take an oath that he did not know,
and he pays for the ox the price of meat cheaply.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא מַאן קָא טָעֵין? מָרֵיהּ דְתוֹרָא קָטָעֵין.

rashi.hebrew: אלא מאן טעין - מי תובע כלום דקאמר (רבא) היכי נידיינו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Gemara's question 'מאן קא טעין' asks who is the plaintiff initiating the lawsuit against the herdsman.

rashi.hebrew: מריה דתורא טעין - ושואל שמתה בהמתו אצלך ותובע את האפוטרופוס:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י identifies the plaintiff as the original owner of the ox, who is suing the herdsman for the death of his animal while in his care.

rashi.hebrew: ספסירא - קונה ומוכר בהמות בשוק ביום שקנה מוכר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines the ספסירא as a middleman who buys and sells animals in the market on the same day.
רש״י explains that the ספסירא takes an oath that he was unaware of the defect in the ox at the time of purchase.

rashi.hebrew: ומשלם בקרא דמי בשר בזול - ותמיה אני מנין לרמי בר חמא דין זה מה לו לספסירא עם הרועה לא שומר שלו הוא ונראה לי דמתניתין (לעיל בבא מציעא דף לה:) דהשוכר הפרה לחבירו והשאילה לאחר ומתה כדרכה דקאמר ר' יוסי יחזיר פרה לבעלים ואוקימנא הלכה כר' יוסי אלמא אע"פ שאין הבעלים בעלי דברים של שואל והשוכר שהוא לו בעל דברים אינו חסר כלום שהרי פטור באונסין אפי' הכי הואיל ויש לו דין על השואל לגבות יעמדו הבעלים הראשונים במקומן וגובין מן השואל ה"נ הואיל ויש ליתומים לגבות מן הרועה שהוא שומר שכר שלהם יעמדו בעלים במקום היתומים וגובין ומיהו אי הוה מפסדי יתומים לא היה עושה פשרה בממון יתומים לשלם בזול דיתמי לאו בני מחילה נינהו אבל עכשיו שהוא מגבה לבעלים וכאן אין פשיעה כל כך דאמר בהדי תוראי אוקימתיה כו' הטיל פשרה ביניהם ולא ישלם כל דמי השור כמות שהוא חי אלא העור יחזיר לבעלים והבשר שמין וישלם שני שלישים דהיכא דאמרינן בגמרא משלם בזול כל זוזא שהוא שש מעה כסף חשבינן בארבעה דנקי בפרק מי שמת (ב"ב דף קמו:):
rashi.takeaway: רש״י derives the ruling that the owner can collect from the herdsman despite the orphans having no loss, based on the principle from a מיתא in בבא מציעא that a lessor stands in the shoes of the lessee to sue a borrower, and explains that the herdsman pays the reduced value of the meat (דמי בשר בזול) as a compromise since the orphans are not at a loss and cannot forgive the debt.

tosafot.hebrew: הכא בספסירא דזבן מהכא וזבן להכא - ומספקא ליה בהא דינא אליבא דר' יוסי דאמר תחזור פרה לבעלים הראשונים אלמא משכיר במקום שוכר קאי ואית ליה דינא בהדי שואל והכא נמי ספסירא במקום יתמי קאי ואית ליה דינא בהדי בקרא כאילו הוי שומר שלו או דלמא לא דמי דהתם ודאי קאי משכיר במקום שוכר דשוכר שייך בגוף הפרה שהרי שכרה אבל גבי יתמי כיון דמקח טעות הוא אין להם בגוף השור מאומה איך יעמוד הספסירא במקומם ומיהו אי לאו דטעין בקרא אנא בהדי תוראי אוקימתיה הוה פשיטא ליה דמיחייב בקרא אע"ג דמקח טעות הוא ולית ליה דינא בהדי ספסירא כיון דפשע חייב דספסירא תובע ליתמי שורו שמת בפשיעה והם שומרים חנם עליו והיתומים יחזרו על הבקרא שיפטרם מן הספסירא כי פושע הוא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions whether the ספסירא can stand in the place of the orphans to sue the herdsman, since the orphans have no claim on the ox itself due to the sale being a מקח טעות, unlike a lessee who has a right to the animal itself.

tosafot.hebrew: דמי בשר בזול - פרש"י הטיל פשרה ביניהם ורבינו תם פירש דדין גמור הוא דאם היה מודיעו היה הספסירא שוחטו מיד ומוכרו בזול ולא היה יכול להשהותו עד יום השוק שהיה מת קודם:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות presents רבינו תם's alternative explanation that the payment of דמי בשר בזול is not a compromise but a strict legal ruling, because if the herdsman had informed the ספסירא immediately, the ספסירא would have slaughtered and sold the meat at a low price right away rather than waiting for the market day.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות challenges רש״י's derivation of the ספסירא's standing to sue based on the orphans' rights, arguing that since the orphans have no claim on the ox due to מקח טעות, the ספסירא cannot stand in their shoes; additionally, תוספות offers רבינו תם's view that the reduced payment is a strict legal obligation rather than רש״י's view of a compromise.

steps.title: The case of the deposited hops
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara presents a case where a bailee's brewer mistakenly uses deposited hops instead of the bailee's own hops.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a complex case of mistaken use of deposited property. This is a setup for a liability puzzle.
steps.deeperAnalysis: A man deposited hops with a friend. The friend had his own hops. He told his brewer to use 'this' pile, but the brewer used the deposited pile. Rav Amram asks how to rule.

keyTerms.meaning: Rav Amram, the sage posing the question

steps.whatToRemember: The core difficulty is that both parties can claim they acted in good faith based on ambiguous instructions.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'כשותא' refers to hops used in brewing, not cheese as sometimes mistranslated.
steps.whyThisMatters: This case explores the limits of liability when instructions are ambiguous and property is consumed.

phrases.aramaic: הָהוּא גַּבְרָא דְּאַפְקֵיד כְּשׁוּתָא גַּבֵּי חַבְרֵיהּ,
phrases.english: That man who deposited hops with his friend,

phrases.aramaic: הֲוָה לֵיהּ לְדִידֵיהּ נָמֵי כַּרְיָא דִכְשׁוּתָא.
phrases.english: He himself also had a pile of hops.
He said to his brewer: Cast from this one.
He went and cast from the other.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַב עַמְרָם: הֵיכִי נְדַיְינוּ דַּיָּינֵי לְהַאי דִּינָא?
phrases.english: Rav Amram said: How should judges judge this judgment?

steps.triggerLanguage: הָהוּא גַבְרָא דְאַפְקֵיד כְּשׁוּתָא גַבֵּי חַבְרֵיהּ...

rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that כשותא refers to hops (הומלו

steps.title: Challenge to the bailee's liability
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explores why the bailee cannot simply be told to pay, as he can claim he gave correct instructions.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara tests the bailee's defense against liability. This is a challenge to the bailee's position.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If we tell the bailee to pay, he can say: I told him to use 'this' pile. I didn't explicitly forbid using the other. My instruction was ambiguous, so I shouldn't be liable.
steps.whatToRemember: The bailee's defense rests on the ambiguity of 'מֵהַאי רְמִי'.
steps.whyThisMatters: This highlights the difficulty of assigning blame when instructions are not explicit prohibitions.

phrases.english: Let us say to him, for himself:
Go pay, he said:
I said to him from this pile.

steps.title: Challenge to the brewer's liability
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explores why the brewer cannot simply be told to pay, as he can claim he wasn't explicitly forbidden from using the other pile.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara tests the brewer's defense against liability. This is a challenge to the brewer's position.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If we tell the brewer to pay, he can say: He didn't tell me 'use this and NOT that'. I thought he was just suggesting, not forbidding. I didn't know he was insistent.

keyTerms.meaning: he did not say to me
do not cast from this [other pile]

steps.whatToRemember: The brewer's defense rests on the lack of an explicit prohibition.
steps.whyThisMatters: This shows that without an explicit prohibition, the agent can claim ignorance of the owner's strict intent.

phrases.english: Let us say to his brewer:
Go pay, he said:

phrases.aramaic: לָא אֲמַר לִי מֵהַאי רְמִי וּמֵהַאי לָא תִּרְמֵי.
phrases.english: He did not say to me: from this cast and from that do not cast.

tosafot.hebrew: מי אמר לי מהאי רמי מהאי לא תרמי - ואם תאמר דבפ' התקבל (גיטין דף סה: ושם ד"ה הא) תני חדא ערב לי בתמרים ועירב לו בגרוגרות עירובו עירוב ותניא אידך אין עירובו עירוב כאן בשלו כאן בשל חבירו וקמפרש דשלו הוי קפידא ואע"ג דלא אמר ליה מהא לא תערב ויש לומר דבשני מינין הוי קפידא אע"ג דלא א"ל מהאי לא תערב וגבי הבא לי מן החלון והביא מן הדלוסקמא (מעילה כא.) דשליח מעל אע"ג דמין אחד הוא התם מיירי שלא היו במקום אחד והכא מיירי שהיו בבית אחד ולא היה לו לידע שאמר דוקא מהאי רמי:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the brewer's defense is valid because the items were in the same location, so he had no reason to know the owner was insistent about using only one specific item, unlike cases where items are in different locations or involve different species where קפידא is assumed.

steps.title: Resolution: The bailee's silence implies consent
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by suggesting that if the bailee's hops were closer, his silence when the brewer took the distant hops implies consent.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara resolves the case by analyzing the bailee's silence. It concludes that his lack of objection reveals consent to the delay.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The bailee's silence functions as a form of consent. By not objecting when the brewer took time to retrieve the distant hops, he implicitly agreed to the arrangement. This consent makes him liable for the subsequent loss.
steps.whatToRemember: Silence in the face of a delay can imply consent.
steps.whyThisMatters: This introduces the concept of 'גַּלֵּי אַדַּעְתֵּיהּ' — revealing one's intent through inaction.

phrases.aramaic: וְאִי דִּשְׁהָא שִׁיעוּר לְאֵיתוֹיֵי לֵיהּ וְלָא
phrases.english: And if the hops were closer to bring to him and he did not
bring them to him –

phrases.aramaic: גַּלִּי אַדַּעְתֵּיהּ דְּנִיחָא לֵיהּ! בִּדְלָא שְׁהָא.
phrases.english: He revealed his mind that it is pleasant to him! Without delay he waited.

rashi.hebrew: ואי דשהא שיעור כו' - גמ' פריך אם היה כרי שלו קרוב ושל פקדון רחוק ושהא השליח שיעור מהלך דרך הקרוב ולא אייתי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's resolution assumes the bailee's hops were closer, and the brewer took the time it would have taken to walk the shorter distance to bring the distant deposited hops without the bailee objecting.

rashi.hebrew: גלי דעתיה - דבעל הבית דניחא ליה שהרי ידע שמשל פקדון הוי ולא מיחה בידו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the bailee's silence reveals his consent because he knew the hops were deposited property and did not object to the brewer taking them.

steps.title: Challenge: What is the actual loss?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks what the actual loss is, since the bailee profits from the hops used in his beer.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara challenges the premise that a loss actually occurred. It questions whether the bailee truly suffered any financial harm.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara asks what actual loss exists if the bailee profited from the hops in his beer. He should simply pay the value of the hops, leaving no net loss. This challenges the need for a complex liability ruling.
steps.whatToRemember: The Gemara questions the existence of a net loss.
steps.whyThisMatters: This highlights the principle that compensation should reflect actual loss, not just technical liability.

phrases.english: Ultimately, ultimately, what loss is there?
And yet he is profiting from it!

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַב סַמָּא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרָבָא: דַּהֲוָה שִׁיכְרָא חַלָּא.
phrases.english: Rav Sama, son of Rava, said: That it was beer that became vinegar.
Rav Ashi said: In a bag,

rashi.hebrew: והא קמשתרשי ליה - והלא הוא נשכר הכשות של פקדון שהטילו בשכר ושלו עומד וישלם משלו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's challenge assumes the bailee benefits from the hops in the beer, so he should simply pay for the hops from his own pocket, leaving no actual loss.
רש״י clarifies that the term חלא means the beer had soured.

rashi.hebrew: בכיסי - שהיתה כישות גרועה וקוצים מעורבין בה דלא השביח השכר כרצונו וקשה בעיניו שהטילוהו בתוכו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that בכיסי refers to a poor quality batch of hops mixed with thorns, which did not improve the beer as desired and was therefore unpleasant to the bailee.

tosafot.hebrew: בכיסי - פרש"י שהיה הכשות מלא קוצים ולא השביח בשכר כ"א מעט וא"ת פשיטא שלא יתן יותר אלא כמו ששוה להטיל בשכר וי"ל דאע"פ שהיו שוים יותר למאכל בהמה לא ישלם אלא מה ששוה לשכר דראוין הם לבהמה כדאמר בהמוציא [יין] (שבת דף עז:) דגמלא זוטרא גנובתיה משום דאכלה כיסי כי היכי דלא לישתרבו בה ור"ח פירש כיסי הוא שם כשות שצריך ריכוך ובלא ריכוך אין שוה להטיל בשכר ולכך לא היה שכר טוב וקמ"ל שלא ישלם אלא כמו ששוה השכר אע"פ שהיו שוים הרבה יותר שבדבר מועט היה נעשה הריכוך:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that according to רש״י, the hops were full of thorns and barely improved the beer, and addresses the question of why the bailee doesn't pay the hops' higher value for animal feed by ruling he only pays their value for beer production, citing a parallel case in שבת about a camel eating thorns to prevent them from sprouting.

rishonim.hebrew: בכיסי - פרש"י שהיה הכשות מלא קוצים ולא השביח בשכר כ"א מעט וא"ת פשיטא שלא יתן יותר אלא כמו ששוה להטיל בשכר וי"ל דאע"פ שהיו שוים יותר למאכל בהמה לא ישלם אלא מה ששוה לשכר דראוין הם לבהמה כדאמר בהמוציא [יין] (שבת דף עז:) דגמלא זוטרא גנובתיה משום דאכלה כיסי כי היכי דלא לישתרבו בה ור"ח פירש כיסי הוא שם כשות שצריך ריכוך ובלא ריכוך אין שוה להטיל בשכר ולכך לא היה שכר טוב וקמ"ל שלא ישלם אלא כמו ששוה השכר אע"פ שהיו שוים הרבה יותר שבדבר מועט היה נעשה הריכוך:

steps.title: Resolution: The hops were mixed with thorns
steps.stepSummary: Rav Ashi resolves that the hops were mixed with thorns, so the bailee only profits the value of the hops minus the thorns.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב אָשֵׁי offers an alternative resolution based on the quality of the hops. He specifies that the hops were mixed with thorns.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The case involves hops mixed with thorns, degrading their value. The bailee only profits the value of the hops minus the thorns. He pays the value of the hops mixed with thorns according to his profit, explaining the loss.
steps.whatToRemember: The loss is real if the product is degraded.
steps.whyThisMatters: This shows that liability is proportional to the actual benefit received.

phrases.english: Ultimately, ultimately, what loss is there?
And isn't he profiting from it!

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַב סַמָּא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרָבָא: דַּהֲוָה שִׁיכְרָא חַלָּא.
phrases.english: Rav Sama, son of Rava, said: That it was beer that became vinegar.

phrases.aramaic: רַב אָשֵׁי אָמַר: בְּכִיסֵי, וּמְשַׁלֵּם לֵיהּ דְּמֵי כִּיסֵי.
phrases.english: Rav Ashi said: In thorns, and he pays him the value of thorns.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַב אָשֵׁי אָמַר: בְּכִיסֵי, וּמְשַׁלֵּם לֵיהּ דְּמֵי כִיסֵי.
